speaker: interviewer
text: 1996. OK, so Molly, we ended the last tape. You got a new passport. What kind of passport did you get?

speaker: interviewee
text: A civil. Before I was like obligated to the army, a sister during a war, she's obligated to be sent anywhere. But as I wanted to go and to find out about my husband, so that was the only way to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got the new passport. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Poland, back to Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know Harry was in Dubno?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He wasn't there. No. No. He-- I came to Dubno, and then I went to Mlynow to my brother-in-law. And I asked him to-- I said, did you know that-- where Harry is? He said that's-- what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Anyways, [? Fania, ?] [? Fania ?] received letters. And she answered them. She answered them of one letter that she was with me together. She did. She was in the same wood, but not a bunker. There she found a boy. And she is married to this-- to the boy. How do you like that? And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but she will know better Harry's address. So we-- [INAUDIBLE] went to call her. [INAUDIBLE] is Gershon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gershon, yes. He went to-- to her house, to her parents' house. She occupied there. And she said the letters aren't there. She can't find them, but she did receive. She thinks it's-- it's-- what did she say, [INAUDIBLE]. She thinks they're [INAUDIBLE]. They're as far away somewhere. Look in the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to-- to write letters all over. Whether they found him in a coal mine, I described it more or less. And I-- like, I opened my heart. I said, he was taking to the road, to the German for work. What I found, I knew that he ran away to Russia. And he must be in Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I lost a big family, almost 200 people, yeah. My mother had seven brothers too. And all of them had children and grandchildren. We were a big family. But I am small, real small. And so anyways, took not too long. I received a telegram. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] I am-- I am happy that I found you and 1,000-- 1,000 ruble. He sent 1,000 ruble. But I was thinking that he needs 1,000 ruble to go out from there. As long as I had his address, I went to a-- to a printing shop. There was a big printing shop. I went to Rowne. I knew that there is, he need it. He-- he-- he couldn't-- I understood that he wanted for me $1,000 to pay someone. He shall be able to come. And from Russia, we were afraid to ride the extra [INAUDIBLE], you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to where that was a-- a unit operated by-- by Russians, you know? I've walked to him, to the main man, and I told him my history. And there were thing-- I mean, the most of what-- what is good. And I said, he is an excellent paper cutter and bookbinder and whatever you want. He-- he-- he learned that in German [NON-ENGLISH] school. I mean, you know, in a school. And he-- he will be working for you. You will see. You will have. Just I need the [NON-ENGLISH]. I mean, I need something that you need him to write. So he said, come tomorrow. I went, and I bought, but this-- this shouldn't even appear, gifts for him. And it was already prepared. He said, you mail that letter. And I mailed that letter according to his address. And he went from there to the-- the devil, you know, to get a release. And he was in the coal mines. Yeah, most would get killed from the coals. So then Harry came where?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: He came and he started the work in--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: --In Rowne. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: He started to work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I-- I found out that there is a way we were all-- always writing. I wrote a-- a-- a few words in the newspaper that I am from there and there. And I'm looking for relatives. I didn't have my uncle's address. But I described where he is from and his name and who's son he is, his father's name and mother's name, everything. Here, I received from him a letter. And I received from New York, from my father's ex where he-- they attended cheder together because I wrote the name of my father all over them. And from where my father came from. And this man, [? Lank, ?] [? Langer, ?] Mr. [? Langer, ?] and he told me exactly like I would see my father and that time it is young. He said the way-- how he-- he took all the, you know, how he learned to study. To-- what is so-- So please write to me what you can-- what I can do for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wrote to him a letter just to find me my mother's brother. I knew that he didn't have no children. And he was married. So I received a letter from him, their exact address in Chicago. So, and we-- we registered. So we told him that we want to go or to-- or to Israel or to the United States. Where it will get first. Because I-- I gave birth to my Rochelle, you know, to my-- I had a little girl, two years old. So wait, wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Rowne, from Rowne, you went to a displaced persons' camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I skipped. I skipped. Now I see. I came from--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowne.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Mlynow, from-- oh, then my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowne with Harry.

speaker: interviewee
text: --husband came, yes. I went-- we went-- oh. We went like-- we went and, you know, like an illegal. We-- we through Czechoslovakia. We had to pay someone. And there-- there-- there we went through the borders with some more. We found more on the way, more people going there. Yeah, I skipped that. I found--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ended up in--

speaker: interviewee
text: We ended up in-- in Germany, in Foehrenwald, the people who are meeting us already.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there for how many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for several years until I received papers from my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had your daughter while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My daughter was born in Germany. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And so then your uncle sent papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- you know, we went to-- to-- actually, my-- my uncle send us papers. And we received papers, I think, from the-- from the Jewish agency too. I don't know how that was. Maybe-- he said that he paid to the Jewish agency. They shall send the papers. I think this is the way because he-- he indicated in his letters because by the end, when we came, you know, so he told us this is the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get to the United States? You arrived here.

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived to the United States in September the 26th. We arrived to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: --New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. And from New York, we came to Chicago to a very small on the west side hotel. And the Jewish agency picked us up. You know. And they gave us one little room and with a crib inside for Rochelle, you know. And I was pregnant with Irving. So then we-- we were living in that hotel, Palomar something hotel, [? Alamark, ?] [? Alamark ?] Hotel, [? Alamark ?] Hotel in Chicago among the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- and when did you give birth to Irving?

speaker: interviewee
text: In February, February the 9th, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your occupations?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time we were living already in an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. You were only in the hotel a few weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hotel, yes. I-- we-- I went by myself with my boy. And Harry went without me. But then I didn't want to fool them. So I went by myself before we had to give them [NON-ENGLISH], so my uncle came. He gave us some money we shall give them.

speaker: interviewer
text: To get the apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I brought the money, so she overlooked the-- the stomach. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: The pregnancy. OK. And then after that, what kind of occupations have you had? What have you done since you have been here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't have the language. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fact, here, my uncle gave-- called a landsman. And that landsman worked on a place where you need-- where you speak all the languages. If you don't have-- the English is OK too. OK, so that family, this man called me. And he said, you were that and that daughter, granddaughter. Oh, I know, I know you, and you, even your father, I remember him, everything from my city. And he invited us to him for dinner with the little baby and with-- with my stomach. [LAUGHS] And by talking there, he said, Molly, you were-- you were born by business. And your father was doing clothing, men's clothing, this and that. He came, maybe in that time where your father came. And he said he works in a big store there on Maxwell Street. So I said, oh, yes, I speak English-- no, Polish and Russian and Yiddish and Hebrew and Czechoslovakian and whatever. But I don't speak. So he said, I'm going to call him right now to his house, the same Friday night. He never-- passed away later on, you know. He was-- if he was in my father's age, so he was an old man. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This man talked to me Jewish on the telephone from his house. And he said that [? Gabler ?] is his name. And his name [? Gable's ?] Clothing. And they used to advertise it on the television, [? Gable's ?] Clothing. We talk all your languages. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you worked as a saleswoman there, and then-- and for a few years. And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Several years, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for them over five years. Then I decided to open a children's wear store on my own. And Harry got a-- a job in a paper cutting job. Before a different kind, he carried bottles of-- you know, on the fourth floor. That was very hard, very hard, but we made it. Thanks God.

speaker: interviewee
text: So and [? Gable's, ?] the-- the-- this man for whom I worked, he-- we didn't have-- we had to put a deposit in the bank in order I shall get credit by the wholesale people for the children's wear. I-- I-- pass-- I found a little store on Lawrence Avenue. And I called up the owner, and I met him at the store, an empty little store where the-- the Albany Park, the bank is. Very near, so I sat there. Albany Park was, in that time, a very good, you know, neighborhood. And the business were good there. So I went to [? Gable's ?] and I said, could you just give me a good word? I shall get credit. So he said, I'm going to call the Exchange National Bank. Tell them to take off on my account $2,000. You just sign it. Just sign it, nothing else. See how people help me? But all of it, I should not complain. I should, you know, and he used to invite us also for dinner sometimes. They liked me. Or no, there were Sundays I used to go. They didn't let me off. I used to go on Maxwell Street to help them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, then in the beginning, what happened, then this man, you know, he-- he said he wanted to see me tomorrow. This was Friday night. Saturday, so I said, OK. I'll go, but I don't talk English. He said, don't worry. I'll give you people who talks your language. Of course there was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came there, a little jacket, and after I fed my children, my baby was already born, my Irving also. And I came there, and we were talking, talking. Then he said to me, now, let's say if I will hire you, what would you do first of all in this kind of a store? So I said, first of all, I have noticed already, you have a children's section, although it's not infant. But still I have to learn the stock. I have to look the stock, go over, and see the sizes and the prices. So he said, Molly, like this, you will be OK in this country. He liked that answer. So and then you had your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for them over five years, plus addition Sundays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you had your own children's store. And then you sold that after a few years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After how many years was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all what two years, maybe over 2 and 1/2 years. I came out with $6,000 net profit.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I invested it in a three flat building with a garden apartment, an older one, and over on Troy Street. I was a good manager. A good--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went into--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just thanks my parents. They taught me how to manage this [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find out what happened to the rest of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wrote letters. I wrote all over letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you find out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out that they are not there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never went there. I was-- I just couldn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've never been back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never been back. I don't know. I was fighting with myself, but I was thinking because my-- my parents had a store, a new store, which they had rebuilded. I didn't wanted to see it because I was afraid maybe they are going already. They will kill me. And my grandmother had a big store, and they had like a lot, a large, large lot, which from there, they are-- you know. And I was thinking, to heck with them. I didn't even indicate it, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and what happened with Yitzhak and Gershon? Did they come over with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the-- to Germany--

speaker: interviewer
text: Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: --together, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then from there to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there to the United States together. In Germany, in DP-- in DP camp, while I was pregnant, they gave us a separate room, you know, because others, they were like two in a room, three strangers, you know? And from my father, for my brother-in-law, they gave a-- a room because they had Gershon. But Gershon wanted to live with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Gershon was living with us in this one room. And Harry was taking him to the [INAUDIBLE] to this Hebrew school. If Gershon didn't want to eat, I prepared breakfast for Gershon. So [INAUDIBLE], we used to call her. So Harry said, well, he didn't want to eat. He wants to go to the school. To the school, he's afraid he will be late. So I said to [INAUDIBLE], I said, [INAUDIBLE], well, Harry told him, if you won't have now the breakfast, then I won't take you to the school. You know what this means? The school was for him, so in order that Harry should take him to the school, he ate the breakfast. And this is why he is now a professor. [LAUGHTER] OK. Now we need to wrap this up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Statistics and economics, how do you like?

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you have one thing that you want people to remember out of your experiences, 50 years later, what would that one thing be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like the people to remember forever [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what the German country, especially included those who followed the instructions of Hitler, they shall remember that forever, what they have done to a decent, good country of Jews. 6 million die-- 6 million people, they destroyed and tortured before destroying and beated them. I saw with my eyes women, older women beating on the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't included that. And I can witness that. Why? Why? Is there a reason? Where was the war? And now they're-- they're-- looking after the Lithuanians, or whatever they are, Hungarians in the-- what is the country now where they are fighting? Bosnia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bosnia. Why to one Bosnia, yes. The whole world is now mailing-- is sending them already checks. I even received a check-- a-- a note I shall support them. Why didn't they do anything for our people, for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: There is England and France and all the other countries. Where they are saying now there was no Holocaust movement? There was a Holocaust, more than a Holocaust. We went through terribly in our life, the-- what we went through. And we can't forget it. Every minute is in my memory. I can't forget days and nights. Now we have another Hitler who stood up and he gave-- gave appreciation. It been a credit to Hitler, he said. Hitler was a great man. Is this a man who shall be a leader in the United States? For those who were slaves, they was slaves. Why didn't they-- they-- why didn't they protest against that expression? Hitler was a great man. It means that he wants to lead a country and-- be a follower of Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said that the Jews soaked the blood from the blacks. This is not true. The Jews were the first people giving them jobs, and I know it. Even when I came, the first job in my house to paint, I asked for a black man. He was Sam, Sam the painter. And he was a black man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- everything what we could, just help the black people. How could they say that we soaked their blood? It isn't-- the Jewish nation is to support people who needs, anyone who's raising his hands for help should be helped. And this, we remember. This is our religion. OK, Molly. We need to wrap it. Thank you very, very much for allowing us to interview you. We really appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Molly, tell us who these people are from left to right.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, from the bottom, is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the left is-- next-- next to the sitting is my mother's sister, Esther was her name. Next to Esther sitting is my grandmother, Sarah Basia Shechter. Next to my grandmother is my mother sitting. In the front of my mother, my mother was Rachel Shechter. Oh, yeah, her maiden name. And my-- the front of my mother is my brother. Israel was his name, Israel. On the second line, standing from the left is my sister, [INAUDIBLE]. That's Pearl. Next to Pearl is my mother's brother, Barel Shechter. Next to Barel is my uncle's ex-wife, Esther. Next to Esther is Devorah. She was my mother's youngest sister. Next to Devorah to my right, is [INAUDIBLE] Shechter. And he was a very, educated and intelligent man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken? Approximately what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken? In Horochow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Horochow, yes. I believe could be-- could be between '35 and '36. Standing is me, by myself, Molly Lewis.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left. Next to me is my mother's brother, Barel. He was the younger brother, Barel Shechter. Next to Barel is a cousin, a first cousin of theirs, my mother's. And she was [? Faye ?] [? Gruber. ?] And next to [? Faye ?] is my mother's brother, which is Zanvel Shechter. He was considered as the most intelligent of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And sitting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sitting in the front of Barel is his wife. And sitting next is [? Hannah. ?] [? Hannah ?] [? Gruber. ?] Which her father and my grandma were brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was taken also in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Horochow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe already-- maybe when I came on a visit. Yeah. This was the late '30s, maybe '38 or something. This is my sister, Pearl. Next to her is her husband Yitzhak Mandelker. And the picture was taken in Poland in the 1930s. This is my wedding picture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got married to Harry Lewis in 1940, June--

speaker: interviewee
text: 15Th.

speaker: interviewee
text: 15Th.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me by myself. That was in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was that, '40-- '44 in Lviv. And that's written by me is [RUSSIAN] in Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] for a-- for a-- to-- I remember-- a remembrance of that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- this picture was taken in Germany as I was in DP camp. And next to me is my nephew. And we were sent to, how do I say-- how-- how was the name of that city? And the others are also refugees, which remained after the World War II.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Rochelle. She was born in Germany in DP camp. In-- in Foehrenwald, near Munich. This is [? Hannah, ?] my mother's cousin in Israel. Next is Yitzhak, my brother-in-law. And next to him is Gershon Mandelkar, which is my sister's son. And next to him is [? Hannah's ?] husband, [? Shmuel. ?] This is Irving, my son. And to the left sitting is my husband, Harry Lewis. And near him, it's me, by myself, Molly. And in the back is my daughter, Rochelle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken when? In July 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in July--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1957 in Chicago. This is our 50th wedding anniversary taken with our son, Irving. This is also our wedding anniversary picture, taking my-- a-- the picture of my daughter, Rochelle, and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband's name is Thomas Smith. And they are 25 years married. This is Sarah, my granddaughter. This is my husband, Harry Lewis. We are married 56 years will be in June of this 1996. And this is Harry Lewis.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm married to Molly for 55 years and six month. She's a wonderful girl. I'm not complaining. She's good to me. She's better to our children. She's a good mother. Very good. Sweet this.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] OK. Good.
